sections.heading: Thermal Imaging Inspection in Amherst, MA
sections.meta: Standalone: $150 standalone scan · As add-on: $95 with home inspection · Thermal images embedded in the inspection report

sections.heading: What Amherst Buyers and Owners Need to Know

blocks.html: Thermal imaging on Amherst triple-deckers finds missing insulation in balloon-framed wall cavities, air leakage at stacked porches, and active knob-and-tube heat signatures invisible in plaster. On East Amherst splits, we map kneewall insulation gaps, attic-bypass leakage at chases, and moisture intrusion at ice-dam locations along the eaves before it shows on the ceiling.

Ken Arnold has been performing thermal imaging inspection services across the Pioneer Valley since 2004 — including hundreds of Amherst-area properties. Every inspection is performed personally by Ken (MA License #587), follows ASHI standards, and is delivered as a comprehensive photo-documented digital report.
Infrared scanning surfaces issues invisible to the naked eye — water intrusion, missing insulation, electrical hot spots, and HVAC performance problems.
For full scope, methodology, and pricing details, see the canonical service page: Thermal Imaging Inspection. For broader context on Amherst's housing stock, see the Amherst town profile.

items.q: What does thermal imaging actually show?
items.a: An infrared camera measures surface temperature differences. That sounds modest, but it's exactly what reveals: missing insulation in walls and ceilings (cold spots in winter), water intrusion behind finished surfaces (cool wet spots), overheated electrical breakers and connections (hot spots), HVAC distribution issues (uneven supply temperatures), and air leakage at penetrations and weatherstripping. None of these are visible to the eye; all of them are obvious on infrared.

items.q: Is this the same as 'energy audit' thermal imaging?
items.a: Same equipment, different focus. An energy audit uses thermal imaging to evaluate envelope performance and identify weatherization upgrades. A home-inspection thermal scan uses it to find safety, structural, and functional defects — water leaks, electrical hot spots, missing insulation that's likely to cause ice damming. Both are useful; they answer different questions.

items.q: When does it need to be a separate appointment?
items.a: Almost never. Thermal imaging is a quick add-on to any home inspection — typically 30–45 minutes of additional time on-site. It's most effective in heating season (Nov–March) when interior/exterior temperature differentials are highest, but cooling-season scans are useful too, particularly for HVAC and water-leak detection.

items.q: Do new construction homes need thermal imaging?
items.a: Yes — possibly more than older homes. Modern envelopes are tightly built and depend on continuous insulation and air-sealing for performance. Missed insulation in a wall cavity, a poorly sealed window, or a HVAC duct in an unconditioned space all show up immediately on thermal. Catching them before drywall (during a pre-drywall walkthrough) is dramatically cheaper than fixing them later.

cards.title: Buyer Home Inspection in Amherst

cards.title: Pre-Listing Home Inspection in Amherst
